definition: Feedback or information specific enough to act on immediately without further clarification.
exampleSentence: Please give actionable feedback, not just that something doesn't feel right.

definition: Shared understanding and agreement among team members on goals, priorities, or direction.
exampleSentence: We need alignment on the deadline before the sprint planning meeting.

definition: Unclear requirements or situations where multiple interpretations are possible.
exampleSentence: The spec was full of ambiguity, so I set up a call to clarify before building.

definition: Exchanging information without expecting an immediate response, common in remote teams.
exampleSentence: Async communication works well for our team across four time zones.

definition: A person's available time and mental capacity to take on additional work.
exampleSentence: I don't have the bandwidth for a new project until next month.

definition: Spending disproportionate time debating trivial details while ignoring more important decisions.
exampleSentence: We spent twenty minutes bike-shedding over the button label before someone called it.

definition: An obstacle preventing progress on a task that requires external resolution to move forward.
exampleSentence: The missing API key is a blocker — I can't test the integration without it.

definition: Standardized, reusable text or code used repeatedly with little or no modification.
exampleSentence: The project generator creates the boilerplate so you can focus on real logic.

definition: A point in a workflow where progress slows because demand exceeds capacity.
exampleSentence: The design review process was the main bottleneck for every feature release.

definition: Genuine support and commitment from stakeholders or team members for a decision or initiative.
exampleSentence: We need buy-in from the engineering lead before committing to this approach.

definition: The regular rhythm of recurring meetings, releases, or communication cycles in a team.
exampleSentence: We set a weekly cadence for design reviews to keep the team in sync.

definition: The total amount of work a team or individual can realistically handle in a given period.
exampleSentence: The team doesn't have capacity to take on more work this sprint.

definition: A warning or qualification that limits or conditions a statement or decision.
exampleSentence: The data looks good, but one caveat is that we only have two weeks of samples.

definition: A curated log documenting notable changes, fixes, and new features across product versions.
exampleSentence: Check the changelog before upgrading to see if any breaking changes were made.

definition: The quality of communication that leaves no room for misinterpretation or confusion.
exampleSentence: The ticket lacked clarity, so the engineer built the wrong thing entirely.

definition: The meeting point of multiple ideas, teams, or processes — also a common team wiki tool.
exampleSentence: The decision was documented in Confluence so everyone could find it later.

definition: General agreement reached by a group, though not necessarily unanimous approval.
exampleSentence: We reached consensus on the approach after a thirty-minute discussion.

definition: Shifting mental focus between different tasks or projects, which reduces deep work quality.
exampleSentence: Too much context switching was making it hard to finish anything deeply.

definition: Involving members from different disciplines — design, engineering, product — working together.
exampleSentence: The feature required a cross-functional team of design, product, and engineering.

definition: A tangible output — a design, document, or feature — produced for a project or client.
exampleSentence: The main deliverable for this sprint is a clickable prototype for user testing.

definition: The measurable difference or change between two states, versions, or data points.
exampleSentence: The delta between last month and this month's retention is seven percent.

definition: A task, team, or system that another piece of work relies on before it can proceed.
exampleSentence: This task has a dependency on the authentication work being done first.

definition: A research phase aimed at understanding user needs and constraints before designing solutions.
exampleSentence: We ran a two-week discovery phase before writing a single line of code.

definition: Using your own product internally before releasing it to identify issues firsthand.
exampleSentence: Dog-fooding the product internally helped us find ten bugs before launch.

definition: Raising an unresolved issue to a higher authority when it cannot be solved at the current level.
exampleSentence: If the vendor doesn't respond by tomorrow, we need to escalate the issue.

definition: An executive or senior leader — often the audience for high-level presentations and summaries.
exampleSentence: The exec team wants a summary, not a deep technical breakdown.

definition: The scope or impact of a system, feature, or team within an organization or codebase.
exampleSentence: We reduced the technical footprint of the service by merging three repositories.

definition: Unnecessary resistance in a process, workflow, or user experience that slows progress.
exampleSentence: There's too much friction in the deploy process — it takes four manual steps.

definition: Comparing the current state to the desired state to identify what needs to change.
exampleSentence: A quick gap analysis showed the new design was missing three key user scenarios.

definition: To gather or accumulate something over time, such as feedback, support, or data.
exampleSentence: The proposal garnered positive feedback from every team it was shared with.

definition: Designed to be understood at a quick look without sustained attention or reading.
exampleSentence: The dashboard needs to be glanceable so managers can check it in thirty seconds.

definition: Broken into fine, detailed levels of control or specificity rather than broad categories.
exampleSentence: We need more granular permissions so editors can't accidentally publish drafts.

definition: A rule or constraint put in place to prevent teams from making harmful or inconsistent decisions.
exampleSentence: We added guardrails in the CI pipeline to block direct commits to main.

definition: A value embedded directly in code or a design rather than stored in a configurable token or variable.
exampleSentence: The hardcoded URL in the config broke everything when the server moved.

definition: An informal advance warning given to someone before something happens.
exampleSentence: Just a heads-up — the deploy is happening at three today, not five.

definition: Describing a broad overview of something without going into implementation details.
exampleSentence: Give me a high-level overview first; we can dig into the details later.

definition: The measurable effect a decision, feature, or change has on users or business outcomes.
exampleSentence: The impact of the change was a twelve percent reduction in support tickets.

definition: A consequence or indirect effect that follows from a decision without being explicitly stated.
exampleSentence: The implication of removing that field is that old clients will break.

definition: Focused on excessive detail at the expense of the bigger picture or strategic thinking.
exampleSentence: Let's step back — we're getting in the weeds and losing the bigger picture.

definition: A structured process for receiving, reviewing, and triaging new requests or projects.
exampleSentence: New feature requests go through an intake process before joining the backlog.

definition: Specialized terminology used within a field that may be unclear to outsiders.
exampleSentence: Avoid jargon in the release notes — not everyone knows what a webhook is.

definition: The initial meeting that formally starts a project, aligning the team on goals and expectations.
exampleSentence: The project kick-off is set for Monday morning with all stakeholders present.

definition: To use an existing resource, system, or advantage strategically to maximize output.
exampleSentence: We can leverage the existing design system instead of building a new one.

definition: Easy wins requiring minimal effort that can be completed quickly for fast visible impact.
exampleSentence: Let's tackle the low-hanging fruit first to show quick wins to the stakeholders.

definition: A significant checkpoint in a project timeline marking the completion of a key phase.
exampleSentence: Reaching the first public beta is a major milestone for the whole team.

definition: To point out very minor issues in code or design, often during review — sometimes labeled as such.
exampleSentence: I'll mark this as a nitpick so it doesn't block the review from being approved.

definition: A guiding metric or vision that defines long-term success for a product or team.
exampleSentence: Our north star metric is the number of active users after thirty days.

definition: An unresolved question or task that was raised but never followed up on or closed.
exampleSentence: There's still an open loop on who owns the migration — can we close that today?

definition: A tool or system that enforces specific conventions and limits how much users can customize.
exampleSentence: The framework is very opinionated, which makes onboarding faster but limits flexibility.

definition: Extra work, time, or cost required beyond the core task — often from process or tooling.
exampleSentence: The approval process adds two days of overhead to every small change.

definition: To take full responsibility for a task, decision, or outcome within a team context.
exampleSentence: Who owns this feature once it ships — product or engineering?

definition: A recurring problem experienced by a user, customer, or team that drives the need for a solution.
exampleSentence: The biggest pain point for users is that logging in takes too many steps.

definition: A documented record of decisions, communications, or changes kept for reference or accountability.
exampleSentence: Always leave a paper trail for decisions made in Slack by copying them to Jira.

definition: A list of topics raised in a meeting that are deferred to be discussed later.
exampleSentence: That's a great point — let's put it in the parking lot for the next meeting.

definition: To make a significant strategic change in direction in response to new information or constraints.
exampleSentence: After the user research, we decided to pivot and focus on the mobile experience.

definition: A retrospective analysis of a project, incident, or failure to extract learnings without blame.
exampleSentence: The postmortem revealed that a missing timeout caused the outage.

definition: A planning exercise imagining a project has failed to identify risks before they occur.
exampleSentence: A pre-mortem helped us spot three risks we hadn't considered in the plan.

definition: Resistance or disagreement expressed in response to a proposal or decision.
exampleSentence: The engineering team gave pushback on the tight timeline for the migration.

definition: A tangent or deeply nested problem that consumes time without advancing the main goal.
exampleSentence: I went down a rabbit hole debugging the wrong file for an hour.

definition: The period during which a new team member or system gradually reaches full productivity.
exampleSentence: The new engineer will need about two weeks to fully ramp-up on the codebase.

definition: The reasoning or justification behind a design decision, technical choice, or strategy.
exampleSentence: Please add the rationale to the PR description so reviewers understand the why.

definition: To improve something incrementally by removing rough edges or adjusting based on feedback.
exampleSentence: We'll refine the estimates once we have more information from the API team.

definition: A recurring team meeting reflecting on what went well, what didn't, and what to improve next.
exampleSentence: In the retrospective, the team agreed that daily standups were taking too long.

definition: A significant obstacle preventing a team from moving forward on a project or goal.
exampleSentence: The legal review is the main roadblock keeping us from launching next week.

definition: The staged or full release of a feature or system to users or internal teams.
exampleSentence: The rollout will be gradual — ten percent of users this week, the rest next week.

definition: Explaining a problem aloud to an object or person to clarify your own thinking and find the solution.
exampleSentence: I found the bug by rubber duck debugging out loud to my teammate.

definition: A quick informal verification that something makes sense before committing further resources.
exampleSentence: Can you do a quick sanity check on these numbers before I send them to the exec?

definition: The gradual, uncontrolled expansion of a project's requirements beyond its original boundaries.
exampleSentence: The project doubled in size due to scope creep from last-minute feature requests.

definition: Formal approval from a stakeholder or decision-maker to proceed with a design or plan.
exampleSentence: We need sign-off from the security team before the data goes into production.

definition: A time-boxed research task undertaken to reduce uncertainty before committing to a solution.
exampleSentence: We did a one-day spike to see if the third-party API would meet our needs.

definition: To bring a hidden issue, insight, or risk into visibility so the team can address it.
exampleSentence: The retro was a good time to surface the tension that had been building in the team.

definition: Communicating difficult feedback or disagreement with sensitivity and awareness of others' feelings.
exampleSentence: She gave tactful feedback that was honest without making anyone defensive.

definition: Accumulated shortcuts or suboptimal code decisions that will require future rework.
exampleSentence: Technical debt from the initial prototype is slowing down every new feature.

definition: To set a fixed maximum duration for a task or discussion to prevent it from overrunning.
exampleSentence: Let's time-box this discussion to fifteen minutes and then make a decision.

definition: Early evidence that a product, feature, or initiative is gaining real user adoption or momentum.
exampleSentence: The beta launched last week and is already getting traction with early users.

definition: A situation where gaining one benefit requires accepting a cost or limitation elsewhere.
exampleSentence: The main trade-off is between a faster build time and a larger bundle size.

definition: To prioritize a list of bugs, tasks, or requests by urgency and impact to decide what to tackle first.
exampleSentence: We triage bug reports every Monday to separate critical issues from minor ones.

definition: The rate at which team members leave and are replaced, affecting knowledge continuity.
exampleSentence: High turnover means a lot of institutional knowledge leaves with every person.

definition: To resolve whatever is preventing a teammate or task from moving forward.
exampleSentence: I'll unblock you as soon as I finish reviewing the PR — give me an hour.

definition: Using more words than necessary — a quality to avoid in both writing and code.
exampleSentence: Cut the verbosity in the error message — users don't need the full stack trace.

definition: How transparent or accessible the status of a project, decision, or metric is to the team.
exampleSentence: Better visibility into the deploy status would help the whole team.

definition: A temporary solution that bypasses a problem without permanently fixing the root cause.
exampleSentence: The workaround fixes the crash for now, but the root cause still needs attention.

definition: Doing a chain of seemingly necessary but tangential tasks before reaching the original goal.
exampleSentence: I started yak shaving when I tried to update one package and ended up refactoring the config.
